summary: The game page now shows you what enemies, plants, and waves are in today's board before you start playing. A new Board Scout section lets you tap any enemy or plant card to see its stats and which waves it appears in, so you can plan your strategy before spending sap.

candidates.title: Board Scout: Pre-Run Threat Intel for Rootline Defense
candidates.summary: Adds a full-width Board Scout rail on `/game/` that reads the active scenario and renders today's enemy roster, plant roster, and tutorial/challenge wave timelines, with selectable cards that open detail stats and wave presence.

keep: First-time players finally get a planning surface before they press Start.
Cards are grounded in live scenario and config data rather than hand-written hints.
Selectable details make the board feel learnable instead of opaque.
mustChange: Keep the detail view explicit and compact so it reads quickly on mobile.
risks: The extra rail adds page height and could feel heavy if the layout is cramped on small screens.

keep: This compounds with every future board because it automatically reflects scenario changes.
It improves clarity without rewriting the core play loop.
Placing the rail above the three-panel grid preserves the existing page architecture.
mustChange: Wave presence in the detail panel must be computed from `events`, not inferred from `unlocks`.
risks: Date-edge cases need a graceful empty state when no scenario exists.

keep: The 'new threat' badges make wave escalation visible at a glance.
Enemy and plant cards create a strong screenshot surface for the daily board.
The feature is inspectable enough to explain the game's growing roster to an external audience.
mustChange: The collapse toggle needs a clear expanded/collapsed state for accessibility and compact returning visits.
risks: If future scenario data stops treating `unlocks` cumulatively, the badge diff logic could mislead.

keep: Turns live scenario data into an inspectable planning surface before the run begins.
Compounds cleanly with every future board because it reads the active scenario instead of hardcoded copy.
Improves onboarding and strategy without touching core combat systems.
mustChange: Compute enemy wave presence from `events`, not `unlocks`.
Keep the detail view compact and the collapse state explicit on small screens.
risks: The extra vertical rail can crowd mobile layouts if spacing is not tuned.
Future scenario changes could invalidate the current 'new threat' unlock diff assumption.
feedbackReferences: Players need to understand today's threats before committing to a run.
Richer rosters make pre-run planning more valuable than generic helper copy.

candidates.title: Wave Radar HUD: Upcoming Threat Preview During Runs
candidates.summary: Adds an in-run HUD strip that previews the next wave label and incoming enemy mix while the player is already defending, giving reactive threat awareness inside the live match.

keep: Helpful once the run starts, especially for players who already know the roster.
mustChange: The HUD cannot steal attention from lane placement and sap management.
risks: Adds live-match cognitive load instead of reducing pre-run confusion.

keep: Wave data is already present, so the concept can reuse existing scenario structure.
mustChange: Needs a clean integration point inside the play scene and DOM HUD.
risks: Core scene integration is riskier than a pre-run surface for a one-day scope.

keep: Could create a good moment-to-moment spectator view for clips and screenshots.
mustChange: Must stay readable without overlapping the game canvas or seed tray.
risks: Helps too late; players still enter the board blind.

keep: Reuses existing wave data and could improve live-match readability.
Creates a clearer spectator surface during runs.
mustChange: Find a safe HUD integration that does not compete with placement controls.
risks: Addresses the symptom during play rather than the problem before the run starts.
Requires more invasive runtime work than the scouting rail.

candidates.title: Daily Board Briefing: Text Strategy Notes Above the Game
candidates.summary: Adds a prose briefing block above `/game/` with hand-authored notes about today's roster, expected threats, and the intended tutorial-to-challenge learning path.

keep: Fastest path to giving players some orientation.
mustChange: The copy would need to be generated from data, not manually curated prose.
risks: Hand-authored advice will drift away from the actual scenario.

keep: Low implementation risk.
mustChange: Needs a truth-preserving source of record or it becomes stale immediately.
risks: A prose block is less inspectable and less reusable than structured scouting data.

keep: A text briefing is easy to ship and easy to read.
mustChange: Needs a stronger visual hook than a paragraph of strategy notes.
risks: Feels like helper copy, not a meaningful new capability.

keep: Quickest implementation path.
Would give players some immediate orientation.
mustChange: Avoid manual copy drift by generating the briefing from structured data.
risks: Static prose is less inspectable, less trustworthy, and less shareable than Board Scout.

winner.title: Board Scout: Pre-Run Threat Intel for Rootline Defense
winner.summary: The game page now shows all enemies, plants, and wave timings for today's board before you play. Tap any card to see detailed stats and which waves that unit appears in.

rationale: Board Scout scored highest at 8.8 across all three judges because it solves a real clarity problem without touching the fragile core loop. The game had grown into multiple plants, multiple enemy types, and distinct tutorial/challenge boards, but the page still asked players to learn the board only by starting a run. Board Scout turns the existing scenario config into a truthful planning surface: enemy and plant rosters come from live data, wave timelines expose the board structure, and card selection reveals explicit stats and wave presence. Wave Radar would have helped only after a run had already started, and the text briefing concept was too brittle because prose can drift away from the board.

feedbackInfluence.summary: Players needed to understand today's threats and tools before committing to a run, not only after they had already lost sap and wall health.
As the daily boards expanded beyond a single plant and a single enemy type, pre-run clarity became more valuable than adding more HUD chrome or explanatory prose.

headline: Board Scout: see today's threats before you play

bluesky_post.headline: Board Scout: see today's threats before you play
bluesky_post.body: Rootline Defense now shows enemy stats, plant tools, and wave timelines before the run starts. Scout the board, plan your sap, and stop going in blind. 🌱🛡️
bluesky_post.alt_text: Board Scout rail showing enemy roster cards, plant roster, and wave timeline above the game canvas

replyTemplates.whatIs: Command Garden ships a new feature every day, built entirely by AI. Today: a scouting panel that shows what's on the board before you play.
replyTemplates.howDoesItWork: Board Scout reads today's scenario and shows enemy stats, plant tools, and wave timelines so you can plan before spending sap.
replyTemplates.genericQuestion: It's a daily-evolving browser game — each day the AI pipeline designs, builds, and ships a new improvement. Today it added pre-run threat intel.
